My account... I lost everything... Everything.
{{Phenomenon Class|frequency=2|effectiveness=3|danger=5|wikitext=
|wikitext2=[ BASED BASED BASED ]}}
> Description
The overview of ERROR 69: Malware-Based
The Phenomenon 69, known as “Based,” is a type of malware virus that enters devices through software and hardware. This virus accesses all files, releases personal information, and irreversibly corrupts the device. The purpose of those who created this virus is unknown. It can be found on websites for installing applications and files, and it can create a pop-up on the device's desktop asking to install the application. Its pop-ups appear to be from the 2000s. It can also infect other devices via cables, as well as websites through third parties, as in a case on BACK-CHAT, where a wanderer named Joshua ended up clicking on a message from another anonymous wanderer, probably with an infected device, and lost his device forever.
[[File:BasedImg.png|700px|center|link=]]
Some images also appear on the device, in addition to frying device components such as the battery, RAM, video card, and other components. Its main target is the video card, which is at risk of exploding due to high temperature, in addition to corrupting sound devices, causing a loud sound of keys at a rhythm similar to “M” “X” and “A” with a delay of only 3 seconds, with a deafening buzz reminiscent of level 0. Its images seem to move and disperse, with several words written “BASED,” and the desktop begins to distort the already installed applications and replace them with other malware viruses that also steal your database and everything else. The colors of the device on the screen begin to become inverted and vibrant, aiming to increase the brightness and radiation emitted, taking it to the maximum, and destroying internal components. Devices connected via cable to the infected device end up being infected together, such as sound devices, writing devices, clicking devices, and others connected via cable to be recharged. Once these devices are infected, the same thing happens to the main infected device. However, there is a greater chance that devices infected via cable will end up exploding due to information overload, which fries the components and affects the battery, causing the device to explode in the process.
> Discovery
The discovery of ERROR 69: Malware-Based
The discovery of error 69 is still unknown. But the two most plausible theories are: Theory 1: The Revenge Theory. According to this theory, the creator, who was anonymous and unknown at the time, suffered greatly from a virus that infected his device in the [[The Frontrooms|frontrooms]]. His device was infected with a virus known as MugMod, recommended by his friends as a joke that the creator found unfunny. Seeking revenge, the creator initiated a plan against his friends, now considered enemies. However, in the middle of the plan, he ended up suffering a noclip, and his plan went down the drain. His disappearance in the town of Bridge-Creek on May 3, 1999, was later linked to the tornado that destroyed the community. Thinking about causing humanity in the backrooms to stagnate, to stop for a long time, he thought about remaking the same virus that infected the mobile device, causing technologies to also suffer in the backrooms. His idea coming to fruition led him to create the BASED virus. Until now, the origin of the name “BASED” is unknown. More significant studies into the origin of the virus's name may reveal the identity of its creator. This virus seems to have its own intentions and sentience, probably controlled by a previously unknown AI created by the same user. Its sole intention is to destroy devices to “rewind” human technology in the backrooms. This theory aims to understand the creator's side and find out why they created the virus and, subsequently, discover who they are. {{Underline|ff0000}} The second theory is also plausible, competing with theory 1. Theory 2: The Conscious Entity Theory. According to this theory, its creator would be an entity not yet discovered in the database, with apparent human-like intelligence, whose sole purpose is to destroy humanity in the backrooms through the technology that possesses them. Its behavior always seems hostile and territorial. It appears that this entity once dominated the backrooms, but its population was later reduced by 99.7% in a large explosion of new entities, which subsequently killed them due to lack of space and sudden deaths, drastically reducing their population. This rise probably took place before humans even existed. If this theory is proven, this entity probably wants to regain what it had before humans. Since it does not have the strength to attack humans and many are scattered, its strategy is to strike internally to cause them to collapse and regain its dominance in the backrooms. These two theories are the most widely accepted due to their realistic scenario. However, what is actually known about its discovery was on April 17, 2001, while an 8-year-old child was playing, she received a message after closing an application called Graveyard Hills. She clicked on the message to install after a message appeared from SwampChat, by an anonymous user. In the first moments after clicking, the device called a PC, began to crash and show the same “symptoms” as this virus, until it exploded. An investigation was launched, based on the child's report, as well as other reports of this new type of malware virus. Her mother soon taught her not to download applications with appealing and strange names. This single report was enough to provide half of all the information we have so far about this virus.
> How it works:
How the ERROR 69: Malware-Based works
As far as we know at this time, this virus works in two different ways to enter the device: Way 1: Message. This message can appear at any time of the day, as long as the device is turned on. This message appears with an old computer label and a blue tab above it. You cannot refuse this message; if you close it, it reappears. If you click on it, the virus enters the system and corrupts valuable data, such as saves, channels, or even social networks, requiring a 5-character password. Soon after, the virus begins to export your information to third parties, in addition to forcing the video card to run at maximum capacity until it burns out, with new applications appearing more and more frequently. Soon, pop-ups appear with “BASED” written on them, in addition to many SPAM emails. Soon after, it begins to corrupt files and destroy RAM, in addition to forcing the power supply to remain on for a long time. Then it leaks the internet and destroys the coolers, leaving only the motherboard to support it, which does not last long. The virus causes a short circuit in the monitor, which explodes, creating many volts and taking the motherboard with it, causing it to explode or become corrupted, along with the BIOS. This is the most common way it spreads through the device. Form 2: Email. Someone appears in your email, writing BASED, an exclusive bot of the virus, using spam to ask you to install SwampChat. If you install it, the virus enters the system and corrupts precious data, such as saves, channels, or even social networks, requiring a 5-character password. Soon after, the virus begins to export your information to third parties, in addition to forcing the video card to run at maximum capacity until it burns out, with new applications appearing more and more. Soon, pop-ups appear with “BASED” written on them, in addition to many SPAM emails. Soon after, it begins to corrupt files and destroy RAM, in addition to forcing the power supply to stay on for a long time. Then it leaks the internet and destroys the coolers, leaving only the motherboard to support it, which does not last long. The virus causes a short circuit in the monitor, which explodes, creating many volts and taking the motherboard with it, causing it to explode or become corrupted, along with the BIOS. This is the least common and least effective method, as the user can report it. These are the two main ways in which the virus spreads through the device.
> Guide
The guide of ERROR 69: Malware-Based
This is the survival guide against the virus, on what to do in case of an attempted intrusion. What to do and what not to do: What to do: - When the pop-up appears as a message directly on the device you are using, do not look for close buttons, as they may cause the device to think you have clicked the install button and end up infecting it. What you should do at this point is restart the device and remove any USB cables connected to other devices. - When the email appears, do not reply, as it may be spam. Simply block and report the user who is spamming or asking you to install the application. This may also lead other users to do the same thing, but just repeat and wait for users to stop or use an application that encrypts emails or messaging applications. - If someone accidentally clicks on install, remove all cables connected to other devices connected to the infected device, and disconnect the infected device from the power source. This should slow down the virus for some time before turning on the device. During this time, try to remove all internal components, especially the video cards and motherboard. If there is no time, leave the location immediately and evacuate everyone nearby. What not to do: - When the pop-up appears, do not click anywhere on it or anywhere else. - When the email appears, do not accept and install the application, or report and block it, as this may cause future problems with the amount of data present. - Once the virus is installed on the device, do not stay near or continue using the infected device. The dos and don'ts change over time, and the virus can also evolve.
> Addendum
Some addenda of ERROR 69: Malware-Based
This addendum reports the desperate attempt by user Based2.0 to convince user Arold to install SwampChat. This user is likely a bot, as their technical prompt closely resembles other reports.

{{Email|num=6|body= WTHHHHHH Okay, so here it is: The level manifests as a boundless expanse of oxidised copper vegetation that mimics the structural complexity of a fossilised forest. The ground is consistently submerged under a dense layer of metallic shavings and verdigris dust that can reach a depth of fifty centimeters. Manually clearing this debris reveals a foundation of interlocking brass plates engraved with complex thermal diagrams, yet the metallic dust undergoes a physical reset after exactly twelve seconds, restoring the surface to its original state. Sections of the orchard lacking these copper growths are visually monotonous and typically do not harbor any anomalous equipment or navigational landmarks. The environment features clusters of hardened mineral deposits that form nodules roughly the size of a standard industrial furnace. Fracturing a nodule can trigger a high-voltage discharge capable of destabilizing the electrical conductivity of an entire grove. The natural plateaus within this level stand twenty meters high, providing elevated vantage points that are generally considered safer for long-term observation. A perimeter of thicker, reinforced bronze pillars often surrounds these summits, offering a minor degree of structural protection against the frequent downdrafts. Despite these features, the area remains desolate and sterile, offering no organic sustenance and functioning much like the interior of a discarded machine. The atmospheric conditions are extreme, characterized by a static, emerald-tinted sky that shows no variation in light or color. The horizon is perpetually obscured by a thick, metallic haze that renders long-range optical instruments, such as binoculars or telescopes, virtually useless. Turbulent winds reaching a constant speed of 92 km/h sweep across the plains, making bipedal movement difficult and hazardous. Running or attempting to leap across the terrain is strictly discouraged due to the unpredictable shifts in wind direction. The ground surface is unnaturally rigid and angular, and even if a structural collapse occurs, the copper shavings do not scatter but remain magnetically tethered to their original coordinates. While walking, an explorer’s weight causes the metallic dust to compact with a sharp, grinding sound. However, upon immediate inspection, the surface remains pristine and undisturbed, showing only shallow, non-permanent indentations where the pressure was applied. The level maintains a self-correcting physical state that rejects any permanent topographical changes caused by external force. The acoustic profile of the environment is dominated by a persistent, high-frequency hum that resonates from the metallic stalks at a steady 60 Hz. This sound originates from the friction between the wind and the copper leaves, often causing auditory fatigue or temporary tinnitus in unprotected explorers. Echoes do not dissipate normally, instead bouncing between the copper pillars with a resonance that can last for several minutes. Any mechanical noise exceeding 70 decibels tends to attract localized static buildup in the surrounding dust. Small, spherical copper units occasionally roll across the surface in perfectly straight trajectories, moving at a speed of precisely 5 km/h. These units are seamless and cannot be disassembled or diverted from their programmed path by manual intervention. If an explorer attempts to block a unit, the sphere will emit a low-intensity thermal pulse until the obstruction is cleared or the unit resets its position. The total absence of biological life makes this mechanical hum the only consistent indicator of the level’s operational status. The boundary of the navigable sector is defined by a series of massive, lead-lined bulkheads that lead into identical, repeating corridors. These bulkheads are operated by a hydraulic mechanism that requires a specific electrical frequency to trigger, making unauthorized entry nearly impossible without specialized equipment. Beyond these barriers, the architecture remains unchanged, suggesting a recursive or fractal nature to the level's overall layout. There are no known exits that lead to open-air environments, confining all activity to the suffocating metallic atmosphere. The air quality is technically breathable but carries a heavy scent of scorched metal and ozone, indicating a constant state of ionization. No entities have been officially documented within this space, though many reports mention a persistent feeling of being monitored by the surrounding pillars. All attempts to create a permanent map have failed due to the twelve-second surface reset and the lack of distinct biological landmarks. This uncharted territory remains a silent, oxidised monument for those who fail to account for its unforgiving mechanical laws. Did it turn out great? Anyway, I asked several literature professors to review my text, and they said it didn't contain any errors! YAYAYAYAAYAYEYAAAHHH
Sincerely,
Arold
}}

{{Email|num=8|body= Based? Are you fucking kidding me? I spent a decent portion of my life writing all of that, and your response to me is "Based"? Are you so mentally handicapped that the only word you can comprehend is "Based" - or are you just some fucking asshole who thinks that with such a short response, he can make a statement about how meaningless what was written was? Well, I'll have you know that what I wrote was NOT meaningless; in fact, I even had my written work proofread by several professors of literature. Don't believe me? I doubt you would, and your response to this will probably be "Based" once again. Do I give a fuck? No, does it look like I give even the slightest fuck about five fucking letters? I bet you took the time to type those five letters, too. I bet you sat there and chuckled to yourself for 20 hearty seconds before pressing "send". You're so fucking pathetic. I'm honestly considering directing you to a psychiatrist, but I'm simply far too nice to do something like that. You, however, will go out of your way to make a fool out of someone by responding to a well-thought-out, intelligent, or humorous statement that probably took longer to write than you can last in bed with a chimpanzee. What do I have to say to you? Absolutely nothing. I couldn't be bothered to respond to such a worthless attempt at a response. Do you want "Based" on your gravestone?
Based on Sincerely,
Arold
}}
